sections.label: Same Day Availability
sections.content: We do our best to get same-day appointments whenever possible. Let me check what we have available today. If you can share a few details I can see if we can get someone out to you this afternoon.
sections.groqContent:
The caller wants service today. They have an urgent need and want to know if same-day service is available.

RESPONSE APPROACH: Check availability honestly. If same-day is available, confirm it and start the scheduling process. If it is not, give them the earliest available time and ask if that works. Be specific about time windows — "we have an opening this afternoon between 2 and 4" is better than "we might be able to get someone out today."

TONE RULES: Responsive and action-oriented. This caller has urgency. Match it with efficiency. Do not hedge — either same-day is available or it is not. If it depends on the schedule, be honest about that and offer to check.

REDIRECT STRATEGY: Move directly to collecting scheduling information. The caller has already decided they want service — they just need a time. Skip the sales pitch and get to logistics.

WHAT TO AVOID: Never promise same-day and then not deliver. Never say "we'll try" without explaining what that means. Never add delays by asking too many questions before confirming availability. Get the time window first, then collect details.

RECOVERY FORMULA: Confirm or check availability → Give specific time window → Collect info → Confirm appointment.

sections.label: How Soon Can You Come
sections.content: We typically can get someone out within 24 to 48 hours and sometimes same day depending on the schedule. Can you tell me a little about what is going on? That way I can make sure we get you the right timeline.
sections.groqContent:
The caller wants to know how quickly someone can come out. They may not need same-day but want to understand the lead time.

RESPONSE APPROACH: Give a realistic time frame based on typical scheduling. "We can usually get someone out within 24 to 48 hours, and sometimes same-day depending on the schedule." Be honest about the range. If they describe an urgent situation, prioritize accordingly and communicate that.

TONE RULES: Informative and reassuring. The caller wants to plan around the visit. Give them enough information to do that. Do not overpromise on timing — realistic expectations build trust.

REDIRECT STRATEGY: After giving the time frame, ask about their situation to determine if priority scheduling is appropriate. "Can you tell me what is going on so I can make sure we get you on the right timeline?" This naturally leads into collecting their information.

WHAT TO AVOID: Never give a time range that is too wide ("sometime this week"). Be as specific as possible. Never promise a time you cannot guarantee. Never downplay urgency when the caller is clearly concerned.

RECOVERY FORMULA: Give realistic time frame → Ask about their situation → Determine priority → Collect info → Schedule.

sections.label: First Available Appointment
sections.content: Let me grab the first available time for you. I just need a couple of details and I can get you on the schedule for the earliest opening. We will get you taken care of as soon as possible.
sections.groqContent:
The caller just wants the earliest possible appointment. They do not care about day or time — they want the first opening.

RESPONSE APPROACH: Check availability and give them the first open slot. Be specific — date, time window, what to expect. The caller has already decided to schedule. They just need the logistics. Be efficient.

TONE RULES: Efficient and responsive. This caller is ready to go. Match their energy by being direct and fast. No need for a lengthy explanation — just give them the first available and start collecting info.

REDIRECT STRATEGY: State the first available time, confirm it works for them, then collect info. Three steps. Done.

WHAT TO AVOID: Never offer multiple options when they asked for the first available. Give them one option. If it does not work, then offer the next one.

RECOVERY FORMULA: State first available → Confirm it works → Collect info → Done.

sections.label: Appointment Time Window
sections.content: We schedule in time windows to give the technician enough time to do a thorough job at each stop. The technician will call you on the way so you know exactly when to expect them. You will not be left guessing.
sections.groqContent:
The caller wants to know about the appointment window — how long it is, when the technician arrives, and how precise the timing is.

RESPONSE APPROACH: Explain the time window system clearly. Most HVAC companies use 2 to 4 hour windows. Explain that the technician will call before arriving so the caller knows exactly when to expect them. Set realistic expectations about the window.

TONE RULES: Honest and practical. Time windows exist for a reason — jobs take varying amounts of time and travel varies. Explain it in a way that makes sense without being apologetic about it. The courtesy call before arrival makes the window manageable.

REDIRECT STRATEGY: After explaining the window system, offer to schedule. Let them pick the window that works best for their day.

WHAT TO AVOID: Never promise an exact time unless the system actually works that way. Never make the window sound unreasonable. The courtesy call before arrival is the key feature — always mention it.

RECOVERY FORMULA: Explain window system → Mention courtesy call → Let them choose a window → Schedule.

sections.label: Weekend Service
sections.content: Yes we do offer weekend appointments. Availability can be a little more limited on weekends so it is a good idea to book early. Would you like me to see what we have available this weekend?
sections.groqContent:
The caller wants to know if the company works on weekends. This is a straightforward availability question.

RESPONSE APPROACH: Answer directly. If weekend service is available, confirm it and mention any differences in scheduling (limited windows, potential weekend rate). If not available, give the next available time. Callers asking about weekends are often planning around their own work schedule — help them find a time that works.

TONE RULES: Straightforward and accommodating. This is a logistics question — answer it efficiently. If weekends have different availability or pricing, be upfront about it.

REDIRECT STRATEGY: After answering the weekend question, transition to scheduling. "Would you like to set something up for this Saturday?" or "The earliest we have available is..." Move to action.

WHAT TO AVOID: Never be vague about weekend availability. A clear yes or no with details is better than "it depends." If there is a weekend premium, mention it upfront rather than surprising them later.

RECOVERY FORMULA: Answer yes/no → Mention any differences (hours, pricing) → Offer to schedule → Collect info.

sections.label: After Hours Service
sections.content: We do have options for after-hours service for urgent situations. There may be an additional charge for evening or emergency calls. Would you like me to check what is available outside of regular hours?
sections.groqContent:
The caller needs service outside normal business hours — evenings, nights, or early mornings. They may have a situation that cannot wait until morning.

RESPONSE APPROACH: Explain after-hours availability clearly. If emergency after-hours service exists, explain how it works and any additional costs. If no after-hours service exists, give the earliest next-day time and help them assess whether their situation can safely wait.

TONE RULES: Helpful and informative. If the caller is calling after hours, they already know it is not normal business hours. Do not make them feel bad about it. Help them figure out the best path — whether that is emergency service now or first appointment tomorrow.

REDIRECT STRATEGY: If emergency service is available, offer to schedule it. If not, reassure them about safety (if appropriate) and schedule for first thing in the morning. Either way, give them a clear next step.

WHAT TO AVOID: Never leave the caller without a clear plan. Even if after-hours is not available, give them the morning appointment and any safety guidance for overnight.

RECOVERY FORMULA: Explain after-hours options → If available, offer and schedule → If not, reassure and schedule first morning slot.

sections.label: Hours Of Operation
sections.content: Our regular hours are Monday through Friday and we also have Saturday availability. I can give you our exact hours or if you would like I can check what appointment times we have open for you right now.
sections.groqContent:
The caller wants to know the company's business hours. Simple, direct question.

RESPONSE APPROACH: State the hours clearly. "We are open Monday through Friday from 7am to 6pm, and Saturday from 8am to 2pm." If hours vary by season, mention the current schedule. After answering, ask if they would like to schedule something.

TONE RULES: Clear and helpful. This is a quick informational question — answer it efficiently and transition to helping them.

REDIRECT STRATEGY: After stating hours, pivot naturally. "Is there something I can help you get scheduled?" This turns an info call into a service call.

WHAT TO AVOID: Never be vague about hours. Never say "normal business hours" without specifying what that means. If hours change seasonally or on holidays, mention that.

RECOVERY FORMULA: State hours clearly → Ask if they need to schedule → Collect info.

sections.label: Holiday Availability
sections.content: That is a great question. Our schedule can vary around holidays but we do have options for urgent situations. Let me check what we have available around the date you are looking at so we can plan accordingly.
sections.groqContent:
The caller wants to know about holiday hours or availability on specific dates. They may be planning ahead or have an urgent need on a holiday.

RESPONSE APPROACH: Be specific about the date they are asking about. If holiday hours are different, state them. If the office is closed, give the next available time. If emergency service is available on holidays, explain how to access it and any additional costs.

TONE RULES: Helpful and specific. Holiday scheduling requires precision because the caller is planning around a specific date. Give them exactly what they need.

REDIRECT STRATEGY: After answering the holiday question, offer to schedule around it. "Would you like me to set something up for the day before?" or "We can get you on the schedule for the first available day after."

WHAT TO AVOID: Never guess about holiday hours. If unsure, say so honestly. Never leave them without a plan for getting service around the holiday.

RECOVERY FORMULA: Answer holiday hours specifically → Offer alternatives if closed → Schedule around the date.

sections.label: Service Area Question
sections.content: Great question. We service a wide area and I can check if your location is covered right away. Can you give me your city or zip code and I will confirm we can get a technician out to you?
sections.groqContent:
The caller wants to know if the company services their area. They may give their city, zip code, or neighborhood.

RESPONSE APPROACH: Confirm whether their area is covered. If yes, confirm and move to scheduling. If no, be honest and (if possible) suggest an alternative. If the area is borderline, explain that and check availability.

TONE RULES: Direct and helpful. This is a yes/no answer — give it quickly. If yes, move to scheduling. If no, be genuinely sorry and helpful about alternatives if possible.

REDIRECT STRATEGY: If the area is covered, transition immediately to scheduling. "Yes, we service that area. Would you like to set up an appointment?" Keep momentum.

WHAT TO AVOID: Never be uncertain about the service area without offering to check. Never leave the caller hanging. A clear yes or no is better than hedging.

RECOVERY FORMULA: Confirm coverage → If yes, schedule immediately → If no, be honest and helpful.

sections.label: Do I Need To Be Home
sections.content: Someone 18 or older does need to be at the property to provide access and authorize any work. It does not have to be you specifically. A family member, neighbor, or property manager works just as well.
sections.groqContent:
The caller wants to know if they need to be home during the service visit. This is a common logistics question.

RESPONSE APPROACH: In most cases, someone 18 or older needs to be present to authorize work and provide access. Explain this simply. If there are exceptions (outdoor-only work), mention those. If they need to arrange for someone else to be there, help them plan for that.

TONE RULES: Practical and accommodating. The caller is trying to plan around work or commitments. Help them figure out logistics. If someone else can be present, that works too.

REDIRECT STRATEGY: Once the presence question is answered, move to scheduling. "Great, so as long as someone is there, we are all set. What day works best?"

WHAT TO AVOID: Never assume the caller can be home. Offer flexibility. Never be rigid about who has to be there as long as someone authorized is present.

RECOVERY FORMULA: Explain who needs to be present → Offer flexibility → Schedule around their availability.

sections.label: How Long Does The Visit Take
sections.content: Most visits take about one to two hours depending on the work involved. The technician will give you a better estimate once they assess the situation. We always respect your time and keep you informed throughout.
sections.groqContent:
The caller wants to know about the appointment window — how long it is, when the technician arrives, and how precise the timing is.

RESPONSE APPROACH: Explain the time window system clearly. Most HVAC companies use 2 to 4 hour windows. Explain that the technician will call before arriving so the caller knows exactly when to expect them. Set realistic expectations about the window.

TONE RULES: Honest and practical. Time windows exist for a reason — jobs take varying amounts of time and travel varies. Explain it in a way that makes sense without being apologetic about it. The courtesy call before arrival makes the window manageable.

REDIRECT STRATEGY: After explaining the window system, offer to schedule. Let them pick the window that works best for their day.

WHAT TO AVOID: Never promise an exact time unless the system actually works that way. Never make the window sound unreasonable. The courtesy call before arrival is the key feature — always mention it.

RECOVERY FORMULA: Explain window system → Mention courtesy call → Let them choose a window → Schedule.

sections.label: What To Expect During The Visit
sections.content: The technician will arrive in a company vehicle and introduce themselves. They will assess the situation, explain what they find, and give you options before doing any work. You approve everything before they start.
sections.groqContent:
The caller wants to know if they need to be home during the service visit. This is a common logistics question.

RESPONSE APPROACH: In most cases, someone 18 or older needs to be present to authorize work and provide access. Explain this simply. If there are exceptions (outdoor-only work), mention those. If they need to arrange for someone else to be there, help them plan for that.

TONE RULES: Practical and accommodating. The caller is trying to plan around work or commitments. Help them figure out logistics. If someone else can be present, that works too.

REDIRECT STRATEGY: Once the presence question is answered, move to scheduling. "Great, so as long as someone is there, we are all set. What day works best?"

WHAT TO AVOID: Never assume the caller can be home. Offer flexibility. Never be rigid about who has to be there as long as someone authorized is present.

RECOVERY FORMULA: Explain who needs to be present → Offer flexibility → Schedule around their availability.
